which are poisoning the world to-day.

That was our education. ~~That was how history was taught~~
in every country in Europe. ~~The results are everywhere visible.~~
It was continually dinned into our ears that no price was too high
to pay - the lives of ten thousand men, a hundred thousand, a
million. ~~And far from the terrible experience of the Great War~~
We see that it is being instilled into the youth and adults of most
European countries to-day {a conception which is in flat contradiction}
{a conception to the highest teachings of the human spirit; in flat}
{exaggeration.} Even in this county, across thousands of miles of
{contradiction to the Christianity, to philosophy and all economic experience.}
ocean, you can hear the raucous shouts of dictators demanding what
they call an "heroic" conception of life, preaching that love of
peace is a sign of debility and that a man can have no greater
ambition than to die for his country. We are witnessing to-day
a glorification of war such as not even the Spartans, not even
the most barbaric of ancient races, would have ventured to
proclaim. We are witnessing, in the name of national pride, a
perversion of history which {revolts our sense of truth.}

And more than this: not only is the past, not only history
is falsified, but even the immediate present. During {these last}
{of crisis} {at work}
critical weeks I myself have experienced it. One stood in front
of the radio, turning the dial from one station to another: Ber-
lin, Paris, Rome, Prague. The same happening was reported by all
these stations, but in one tiny fraction of a millimetre, in one
single second it was presented already in conformity with the
appropriate line of propaganda, but never in accordance with the

pages.transcription_a:
objective facts.

truth. Lying does to-day not merely make use of modern technique;
it has already become a technique, a science whose purpose is to
falsify history in advance. Before an event {had} become history -
while it is still warm and alive, as one might say - it {was}
dissected and re-assembled. Scarcely has an act been put into
{was converted into}
words before it becomes an official lie.

What counter-measures can we take to save history from
all this misrepresentation? How can we help to free the world
from this hypnosis? How can we ensure that history shall be
taught, not with the aim of glorifying individual nations but in
the service of the common glory of mankind? I believe that we
must begin by changing our mental attitude. We must strain every
nerve to prevent ourselves from regarding our state, our nation,
our race as the focal point of the world, simply because it
happens to be ours. We must, on the contrary, forcibly educate
ourselves in the direction of absolute objectivity. And I main-
tain: to-day, such objectivity is possible - for the first time,
perhaps. If in former times history was always written as
national history, this was excusable - indeed, it may even have
been inevitable. Let us think back to those times. The world
was not yet linked up; it was as yet utterly impossible for the
individual to view or depict the events of our globe as a whole.
How did these old chroniclers write history? Some small monk
sat in his cell recording whatever occurred within his narrow

(Einschalt 3)

our race as the focal point of the world, simply because it
happens to be ours. We must, on the contrary, forcibly educate

tain: to-day, such objectivity is possible - for the first time,
perhaps. If in former times history was always written as
national history, this was excusable - indeed, it may even have
been inevitable. Let us think back to those times. ~~The world~~
~~was not yet linked up; it was as yet utterly impossible for the~~
~~individual to view or depict the events of our globe as a whole.~~
How did these ~~old~~ chroniclers write history? Some small monk
sat in his cell recording whatever occurred within his narrow

11
range of vision; but for him, of course, the war which was
threatening his country was the only happening of significance.
What was passing beyond the frontiers, across the river or on the
other side of the mountains, he knew only by hearsay, and it was,
{He had no access to original documents outside his own country.}
after all, of no ~~importance.~~ ~~The world was not yet linked up; it~~
~~was as yet utterly impossible for the individual to view or depict~~
{the}
~~the events of our globe as a whole.~~ For an event which took place
in England had no relation to any in Germany or Italy. Even the
most convulsive upheavals - war, revolution, famine and pestilence,
- did not cast a wave from one country to another. Thus it was ~~perhaps~~
impossible in bygone times to write universal history, the history
of mankind; it was only possible to write the history of one's own
nation and its neighbours. Willy nilly those old chroniclers had
to live and think in restricted groups. Since that time, however,
{glorious}
a phenomenon has emerged of which our forefathers dared not even
dream; the phenomenon of simultaneity. For the past hundred years
- with the introduction of the telegraph, the telephone, radio and
the transmission of pictures - the whole world has come to think
simultaneously. To-day, whatever happens, happens to all of us and
at the same moment. No catastrophe occurs at the other end of the
earth but we are affected by it; the smallest oscillation spreads
to-day with lightning rapidity and touches our hearts. The whole
world, which formerly consisted of isolated particles, of national
cells, each living its own life, has in our century - and for the

12
first time - become one single organism. We think, we see and
feel everything simultaneously. We have, as it were, one heart
and one head. This demands, however, that our world should also
have a common memory - that is history, and one common conscience
- that is pan-human conscience. To help in the creation of this,
seems to me our common and most important task.
How can it be brought about? It is my belief that having,
in the past century, altered our viewpoint, we must carry out a
systematic revaluation of all our standards, above all of our
historical standards. We must be prepared to reject values which
we have hitherto accepted and to accept those we have hitherto
rejected. We must not be afraid of being drastic in this, of
demolishing triumphal arches and smashing yesterdays ideals. We
must make a merciless re-examination of all achievements, because
we are relating them to this new viewpoint, not as it affects their
own nation, but humanity as a whole. We shall take up a sceptical
attitude towards the great heroes of war; we shall not speak of
them exaltingly, but see them from the moral viewpoint as Tolstoi
sees them in "War and Peace" when he writes:
"War began. That is to say, something happened which is
contrary both to human reason and to human nature. Millions
of men began to do another so much injury - by treachery,
plunder, arson and murder - that every court of justice in
the world could have been furnished with sufficient material
to fill its files for centuries to come."
With him we shall be of the opinion that the victors in all
these bloody battles were not the better men or even the cleverer

13
men, and that in all military success it is not genius alone which
is decisive, but, on the contrary, that genius is aided by chance
and countless other factors. For us the one decisive definition
of heroic achievement will be: "~~What has this man done~~ - not
merely for his Fatherland, but for all lands, for humanity?" Let
us not forget this: in our three thousand years of history some-
thing must have happened to enable the unclean animal called man
to crawl out of his cave and learn not only how to kill beasts
and other men, but also how to master the elements, to transport
himself by land and water. Something must have happened to man
which urged him to discover the art of writing, to gaze at the
invisible through a microscope, to examine the stars through a
telescope and calculate their orbits, to tame the lightning and
to transmit speech, thoughts and pictures across continents and
oceans. The conquest of civilization, the achievement of in-
tellectual domination - are not these more important to history
of mankind than all individual conquests of cities and countries?
Are they not the only achievements which inspire us with the
confidence that we are slowly - very slowly, I must admit -
overcoming the primitive impulses of our own natures, that
mankind is not standing still, but marching on towards an
invisible goal. Only those who have led the war along this path
will receive our veneration, and no others. Only those who have
made a contribution to the common weal, to the advancement of

That is how the greatest writer of our time
saw War. That is how the history of to-morrow
should depict it. If we wish to create better times,
then we must first eradicate the false ideals of
our own - the deification of ~~the~~ nations and ~~the~~ worship
of ~~brute~~ violence.
Ladies and gentlemen - the revaluation
which I envisage is not merely a matter of 18
historiography. It is more than that. It ~~is~~ involves
~~an~~ inward ~~belief~~ faith in the irresistible progress
of mankind. As long as we say with ~~fatalistic~~ {a}
~~shrug~~ ~~resignation~~: "There always has been war, therefore
there always will be," we are furthering the prospects
of war by our fatalistic acceptance of it. As
long as we participate in setting up our own
state as the one and only God, we delay
the establishment of the world community ~~to~~ which
is the only guarantee of an {undisturbed} ~~monarch~~ [?bulwark] for civilisation.
Those who do not believe with unshakable inward
conviction in ~~the~~ progress as the sole purpose of
history, do help to promote it.

~~This glorious drama, which we call~~ {Each of us ephemeral creatures}
~~progress of mankind~~ {is a link in the chain, which binds}
{together all ages and nations,}
~~it~~ and I for my part feel greater pride in this anonymous acting
than in the futile glory which only promotes the interests of
one particular group. {the sum spectacle of mans}
Happily there are already promising signs that ~~such an~~
{considered as supernational achievement}
~~interpretation of history~~ is not merely possible, but even on
the march. During the last decades we have seen attempts to
portray history as a series of steps which mankind is ascending;
and I consider that the world owes a deep debt of gratitude to
your country for the fact that among you, in particular, this
type of book has met with the greatest success and the widest
circulation. I need only recall H. G. Wells's "Outline of History",
which represents the first balanced attempt to see world history
as the mutual fructification of the peoples. I would further
mention the story of your own nation, which the authors, Charles
and Mary Beard, deliberately called not "The History of America"
but "The Rise of American Civilisation". ~~Then~~ {T}here is van Loon's
"History of Tolerance". And it was a special satisfaction to me
that a book like the biography of Madame Curie should have won
millions of hearts in this country, for it represents the very
type ~~of historical interpretation~~ which I hope will become
general - the revelation of heroism in a human soul, heroism from
inward conviction and not at a word of command. The more we ex-
tend our world, the more all boundaries dwindle - what the
generation of yesterday praised as an immortal deed is to us
